entities.description: The composite discovery unit encoded by CME00106: a short Parvus Cato of disputed attribution followed by Burgh's much longer Cato Major. It is neither a physical witness nor Förster's reconstructed text.

entities.description: The short Middle English component cataloged by DIMEV 6321 / NIMEV 3955. Förster prints seven stanzas from Hh.4.12 but doubts that Burgh composed or translated it.
entities.label: Parvus Cato
notes: Burgh is a disputed attributed creator for this component, not a confirmed author.

rights.basis: The medieval text predates modern copyright terms by centuries; this does not determine rights in any modern representation.

entities.description: Benedict Burgh's four-book, 161-stanza Middle English rhyme-royal adaptation of the Disticha Catonis, cataloged by DIMEV 1418 / NIMEV 854.

rights.basis: The fifteenth-century text predates modern copyright terms by centuries; this does not determine rights in any modern representation.

entities.description: The late-antique Latin moral school text adapted in both Middle English components. Burgh's exact Latin exemplar is unidentified; Baehrens's 1881 edition is only Förster's modern numbering and comparison source.

bibliographic.title: Die Burghsche Cato-Paraphrase

entities.description: One eclectic editorial project published in two Archiv installments. Förster chooses Hh.4.12 for Parvus and Rawl. C.48 for Major, collates the historically listed witness set and early prints directly or through supplied transcripts and collations, and provides an extensive German glossary.
entities.label: Max Förster's Die Burghsche Cato-Paraphrase editorial project

entities.description: The earliest currently distinguished Caxton edition, circa 1476, represented by the Huntington copy R.B.62364. It is not STC 4850, the separate edition Förster collated.

entities.description: A distinct circa-1477 Caxton edition surviving uniquely in Cambridge University Library AB.8.48.2. This is Förster's collated 'first Caxton' and Jenkinson's 1906 facsimile source.
entities.label: William Caxton's Burgh Cato, STC 4850

entities.description: A third distinct Caxton edition, circa 1483; it must not be conflated with either STC 4850 or STC 4851.
entities.label: William Caxton's Burgh Cato, STC 4852

entities.description: The London 1557/1558 print collated by Förster. It reverses the medieval component order to Major then Parvus and omits the Parvus dedication.

entities.description: The current catalog explicitly identifies Peniarth 481 as former Ancient Peniarth 38, resolving Förster's P shelfmark. Parvus begins f. 1r and Major f. 2r; visual inspection finds the complete Cato ending at f. 27r. Förster had not seen P.

notes: The original manuscript is restricted, but its complete digital surrogate is public.
The original velvet-covered wooden boards survive.

next_steps: Consult a modern critical study or edition devoted to DIMEV 6321.
Compare complete Parvus witnesses and their Latin framing without treating Förster's Hh.4.12 base as an authorial original.
openQuestions.question: Was the Parvus Cato composed or translated by Benedict Burgh, or independently appended to the Cato Major tradition?

next_steps: Trace Raine-collection ownership and sale records after Förster.
Do not adopt DIMEV's tentative Peniarth 481 association unless the conflict with Peniarth 481's confirmed former identity as Ancient Peniarth 38, Förster's separate P, is resolved.
openQuestions.question: What is the present identity and location of Förster's Raine manuscript Y?

next_steps: Inspect Kuriyagawa's 1974 edition and later textual studies in full.
Keep DIMEV-only additions collateral unless a source explicitly demonstrates Förster's knowledge or use of them.
openQuestions.question: What current stemma and critical text best refine or supersede Förster's reconstruction across the enlarged DIMEV witness census?

next_steps: Request whole-codex reproductions from Cambridge University Library and the Bodleian, including covers, boards, pastedowns, flyleaves, every folio, blanks, stubs, and edges where feasible.
Record delivered-file terms separately, map Rawl. C.48 ff. 84r–111v, and reconcile DIMEV's Hh.4.12 loci with Förster's one-folio-later Parvus milestones inside the complete objects.
openQuestions.question: Can complete color facsimiles be obtained for Förster's Hh.4.12 Parvus base and Rawl. C.48 Major base?

next_steps: Search Förster's papers and correspondence for the supplied transcript and collation.
If found, replace the provisional catalog-description modeling with a dedicated intermediary type when the lineage schema supports one.
notes: Their historical use is secure from Förster's statement; only present survival and location are unknown.
openQuestions.question: Do A. Napier's Rawl. poet. 32 transcript and William Greenwell's Durham collation survive, and where are they held?

relations.subjectLabel: Max Förster's Die Burghsche Cato-Paraphrase editorial project

reviewNotes: Förster's serial article is represented as one editorial project containing two separately citable installments.
The short Parvus Cato and long Cato Major have separate DIMEV records and separate attribution evidence; their consecutive printing does not make Burgh's authorship of both secure.
Napier's Rawlinson transcript and Greenwell's Durham collation are modeled provisionally as catalog descriptions because the schema has no dedicated editorial-intermediary entity type.
Modern DIMEV additions are collateral census witnesses unless a Förster relation is independently evidenced.

lineage.summary: CME00106 is the checked-in copy of the University of Michigan encoding of Max Förster's eclectic Middle English edition, published in two separately paginated Archiv installments in 1905 and 1906. Förster prints a seven-stanza Parvus Cato from Cambridge University Library MS Hh.4.12 and a 161-stanza Cato Major based on Bodleian MS. Rawl. C. 48 but emended 236 times from a broad witness and early-print collation; his German glossary follows. The composite work model preserves the uncertain authorship of the Parvus, the four-book Burgh Cato Major, the Latin Disticha source, the physical manuscripts, editorial intermediaries, print editions, and digital surrogates as distinct entities. DIMEV v1.0 supplies a current collateral census and must not be read backward into Förster's source set. Complete public IIIF objects for Cambridge Ff.1.6, Durham Cosin V.ii.14, Göttingen 8 Cod. Ms. philol. 163 n, and NLW Peniarth 481 are cached as whole-object bundles with work loci; neither of Förster's two base witnesses has a complete public facsimile verified here.

catalogSummary.author: Benedict Burgh; attribution disputed for Parvus Cato
catalogSummary.date: Cato Major probably composed or translated 1433–1440; Parvus Cato attribution and date unresolved
catalogSummary.region: England; no narrower work-level place asserted
catalogSummary.title: Benedict Burgh's Cato Paraphrase

catalogingSubject.label: Benedict Burgh's Cato Paraphrase
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the editorially joined Middle English Cato sequence printed by Max Förster and encoded as CME00106: the seven-stanza Parvus Cato, whose attribution to Benedict Burgh is disputed, followed by Burgh's Cato Major, a four-book rhyme-royal adaptation of the Latin Disticha Catonis. The two Middle English components have separate DIMEV records and distinct attribution evidence, so they are retained as parts of a composite cataloging unit rather than flattened into one securely authored work. The subject excludes the Latin source work, every physical witness and early print, Förster's eclectic edition and glossary, the University of Michigan encoding, and the checked-in XML artifact.

contentParts.label: Parvus Cato

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[1]/DIV2[1], LG 1–7; verse milestones 1–50; the following colophon is a non-verse L

notes: DIMEV record 6321 describes this component as 1,185 lines, but that figure belongs to the full Parvus-plus-Major sequence and is internally inconsistent with the record's own short loci. It is not used as the Parvus extent.
contentParts.summary: A short sequence of elementary moral precepts in six rhyme-royal stanzas and an eight-line envoy. Förster prints it from Cambridge University Library MS Hh.4.12 and explicitly questions Burgh's authorship.

contentParts.label: Cato Major

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[1], from DIV2[1]/LG[8] through DIV2[4]/LG[50]; stanzas VIII–CLXVIII, verse lines 51–1185

contentParts.summary: Burgh's four-book Middle English rhyme-royal adaptation of the Disticha Catonis, beginning at stanza VIII and ending at stanza CLXVIII with three eight-line envoy stanzas.

contentParts.label: Förster's lexical glossary
notes: This part is represented for content navigation and edition boundary control. It is not counted as one of the two medieval work items.
contentParts.summary: German editorial notes on Middle English words and forms, retained by U-M after the verse but excluded from the medieval work extent.

assertion.notes: A work-level scholarly estimate, not a date copied from any one witness.

dateStatements.display: Probably 1433–1440, composed for the young William Bourchier

assertion.notes: This is the earliest broad production window among the currently cataloged witnesses directly verified in this research pass, not proof that no surviving witness is earlier.

dateStatements.display: Earliest securely cataloged witness window verified here: mid-fifteenth century, Durham Cosin MS V.ii.14

assertion.notes: This is a print-publication statement, not a claim that the translation was composed for print. The relative order and dates of the three Caxton editions have changed since Förster.

dateStatements.display: Earliest currently distinguished printed edition: Caxton STC 4851, circa 1476

assertion.notes: Earlier manuscript and print witnesses transmit the work; this statement identifies the first complete critical edition verified here, not first textual publication.

extent.description: Three encoded content parts are represented for navigation: two medieval verse works and one editorial prose glossary. Parvus Cato has seven stanzas and 50 verse lines (six seven-line stanzas plus an eight-line envoy); Cato Major has 161 stanzas and 1,135 verse lines across four books. The repository XML records 168 LG and 1,186 L because the Parvus colophon is encoded as an additional L. The 82-paragraph German glossary counts as the one prose content part but is not a third medieval work.

formStatements.description: Predominantly seven-line rhyme-royal stanzas (ababbcc), with an eight-line Parvus envoy and three eight-line ababbcbc Cato Major envoy stanzas.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The Latin Disticha is a related source work and often accompanies the English stanzas in witnesses. Förster's German study, Latin apparatus, and U-M's retained German glossary are edition layers, not primary languages of the cataloged Middle English composite.
The work-level Middle English language and the German, Latin, and editorial layers of Förster's edition are deliberately separated.

Förster silently modernizes punctuation, regularizes capitalization, prints abbreviation expansions in italics, and emends Rawlinson C.48 extensively from a broad collation; CME00106 is therefore not a diplomatic witness transcript.
The four complete public manuscript facsimiles cached for this research are supporting witnesses, not proof that those codices directly supplied U-M's encoding. U-M encoded Förster's printed edition.

Peniarth 481's current catalog explicitly records the historical designation Ancient Peniarth MS 38. That evidence identifies it with Förster's P, which he had not seen, while leaving his separate Raine manuscript Y unresolved.

next_steps: Consult a modern critical study or edition that separately analyzes DIMEV 6321.
Compare the complete Parvus witnesses and their Latin framing without treating Förster's Hh.4.12 copy as an authorial original.
openQuestions.question: Was the Parvus Cato composed or translated by Benedict Burgh, or was it independently appended to the Cato Major tradition?

next_steps: Trace the Raine manuscript's ownership after Förster and inspect York and National Library of Wales accession evidence.
Do not adopt DIMEV's tentative 'perhaps Peniarth 481' suggestion without resolving the conflict that current Peniarth 481 is explicitly former Ancient Peniarth MS 38, Förster's separate siglum P.
openQuestions.question: What is the present identity and location of Förster's Raine manuscript Y?

next_steps: Consult Kuriyagawa's 1974 Caxton-based edition and later textual studies.
Keep DIMEV-only census additions collateral to Förster unless a source explicitly shows that he knew or used them.
openQuestions.question: What current stemma and critical text best supersede or refine Förster's 1905–1906 reconstruction across the enlarged DIMEV witness census?

next_steps: Request whole-codex reproductions from Cambridge University Library and the Bodleian, including covers, boards, pastedowns, flyleaves, every folio, blanks, stubs, and edges where practical.
Record delivered-file terms separately from medieval-text copyright, map Rawl. C. 48 ff. 84–111v, and reconcile DIMEV's Hh.4.12 ff. 28v–30 with Förster's fols. 29v–31r within the complete objects.
openQuestions.question: Can complete color facsimiles be obtained for Förster's two base witnesses, Cambridge University Library MS Hh.4.12 and Bodleian MS. Rawl. C. 48?

notes: The role is work-level translation and adaptation, not scribal responsibility for any surviving witness.

assertion.notes: DIMEV catalogs the component under Burgh, but Förster says the attribution is uncertain and notes that the Parvus is absent from his reconstructed alpha branch.

scope.description: The 1905–1906 immediate source edition, not authorship of the medieval works

subjects.label: Moral advice and conduct of life
Virtue and vice
notes: The work's practical moral topics include prudence, speech, friendship, household conduct, work, wealth, adversity, and reputation.

summaries.value: A Middle English verse adaptation of the moral schoolbook traditionally called the Disticha Catonis, presented as instruction from an elder to a younger reader on worship, family, learning, speech, friendship, anger, household management, work, money, adversity, reputation, and virtuous conduct. The sequence joins a short Parvus Cato of seven stanzas and an envoy to Benedict Burgh's much longer Cato Major, arranged in four books and ending with three envoy stanzas. Förster probably dates the major adaptation to 1433–1440 and connects it with the education of William Bourchier. The Parvus attribution remains uncertain. CME00106 encodes Förster's 1905–1906 eclectic text and German glossary, not a diplomatic transcript of any one medieval witness.

DIMEV separates Parvus Cato (6321 / NIMEV 3955) from Cato Major (1418 / NIMEV 854). Förster prints them consecutively but says the Parvus is absent from his alpha branch, is appended in Cambridge University Library MS Hh.4.12, and may not be by Burgh. The General Corpus record therefore uses one composite discovery unit with two explicit medieval work parts and does not treat the attribution as uniform.

The XML has 168 LG and 1,186 L elements. Its medieval verse extent is 168 stanzas and 1,185 verse lines: the extra L element carries the non-verse colophon 'Explicit liber parui Catonis' after stanza VII. The encoding begins at Förster's printed p. 303, omitting the article introduction on pp. 298–302; it continues through p. 323 of volume 115 and pp. 25–40 of volume 116, including Förster's editorial glossary on pp. 35–40.

assertion.notes: A normalized discovery title based on Förster's established German title. It does not erase the disputed attribution of the Parvus component.

scope.description: Förster's two-installment article and edition title, not a medieval title
DIMEV normalized incipit; witness spellings vary

titles.value: When I advertise in my remembrance / And see how fell

scope.description: DIMEV normalized incipit; witness spellings vary

titles.value: For why that God is inwardly the wit / Of man

work.author: Benedict Burgh; attribution disputed for Parvus Cato
work.dateDisplay: Cato Major probably composed or translated 1433–1440; Parvus Cato attribution and date unresolved

subjects.label: Moral advice and conduct of life

work.summary: A Middle English verse adaptation of the moral schoolbook traditionally called the Disticha Catonis, presented as instruction from an elder to a younger reader on worship, family, learning, speech, friendship, anger, household management, work, money, adversity, reputation, and virtuous conduct. The sequence joins a short Parvus Cato of seven stanzas and an envoy to Benedict Burgh's much longer Cato Major, arranged in four books and ending with three envoy stanzas. Förster probably dates the major adaptation to 1433–1440 and connects it with the education of William Bourchier. The Parvus attribution remains uncertain. CME00106 encodes Förster's 1905–1906 eclectic text and German glossary, not a diplomatic transcript of any one medieval witness.
